exemplar.title: M25 #2: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #2: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Sustained, explicit focus on the prescribed title throughout the essay
Effective linkage to two areas of knowledge (the arts and the natural sciences) using discipline-specific examples
Clear, coherent argumentation with logical sequencing and multiple perspectives acknowledged
Relevant illustrations (e.g., polychrome sculpture, ballet positions, Lamarckism, Newtonian mechanics) that illuminate how âreverenceâ and âfragilityâ manifest differently
weaknesses: Some passages remain largely descriptive rather than probing underlying methods or ways of knowing
Key concepts (ârevered,â âfragileâ) are invoked but not rigorously defined, reducing conceptual clarity
Occasional factual inaccuracies and lapses in precision (e.g., dating errors, misnaming âtheory of relativismâ), and limited citation range
Knowledge questions are not always explicitly formulated to structure each example, which weakens analytical depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay demonstrably fulfills the descriptors of Band 7â8: it maintains a sustained focus on the title, links effectively to chosen areas of knowledge, constructs clear and coherent arguments supported by specific examples, and shows awareness of differing viewpoints. However, recurring descriptive tendencies, underdeveloped conceptual definitions, occasional factual imprecision and missing explicit knowledge questions prevent elevation into the very highest band. On balance, a final mark of 7 accurately reflects the solid critical engagement and structural coherence while acknowledging these analytical and precision-related shortcomings.

annotations.comment: The transition into Lamarckâs theory lacks an explicit link to the promptâs notion of fragility. The student should frame Lamarckâs example as a case study of methodological vulnerability.

The explanation of Lamarckism is simplistic and omits discussion of its empirical foundations and contemporary critiques. Exploring how methodological limitations led to its decline would deepen the analysis.

While the historical examples of spontaneous generation and the Ã¦ther are illustrative, this section remains largely descriptive. The student should formulate explicit knowledge questions to link these examples more directly to the prompt and to frame subsequent analysis.
The diagram repeats âFig.2â and blurs referents from the previous figure, causing confusion. Consistent numbering and clearer captions would improve coherence of visual evidence.

The Newton section begins without articulating a knowledge question to guide critique. Introducing a focused question about the limits of classical mechanics would structure the analysis more effectively.

The discussion of fragility as fear of being wrong is presented without linking to ways of knowing or underlying assumptions. The student should clarify how the fragility of knowledge arises methodologically to deepen the analysis.
The claim that Mark Abbe discovered pigments in âthe year 200â is factually inaccurate and uncited. The student should verify dates and cite archaeological sources to ensure precision.

The student quotes the prompt but does not define the key terms âreveredâ and âfragile,â which reduces conceptual clarity. They should establish precise working definitions at the outset to guide analysis and ensure consistent usage throughout the essay.

The thesis outlines exploration through two areas of knowledge but omits specific knowledge questions for each. The student should articulate distinct questions for the arts and natural sciences to structure a clearer comparative evaluation.

The student asks how respected knowledge needs to be to be revered without operationalizing ârevered.â Defining this term with reference to knowledge criteria or thresholds would strengthen conceptual depth and focus the discussion.

Describing the positions as emblematic of âperfect balanceâ is evaluative without critical scrutiny. The student should analyze what criteria define âperfectâ and consider alternative standards.

The reference to Rameauâs text lacks discussion of its epistemic authority. Examining the cultural context and reception of Le MaÃ®tre Ã  danser would enrich understanding of why the knowledge persists.

The opening overview of art remains at a descriptive level and lacks engagement with knowledge issues. The student should integrate discussion of ways of knowing or methodologies to connect art practices to the prompt.

The header signals a shift to sculptures but lacks an explicit link to the essayâs knowledge question. The student should indicate why this example illustrates the fragility of revered knowledge in art.

While summarizing examples, the conclusion does not consider the broader implications for knowledge production. Reflecting on how fragility shapes scientific and artistic communities would deepen the evaluation.
The final meta-question about all knowledgeâs fragility is provocative but unanchored. The student should outline a theoretical framework (e.g., Kuhnâs paradigm shifts) to explore this claim.

The section introduces Greek and Roman sculptures but does not probe the art-historical methods behind pigment analysis. Discussing how these methods generate knowledge would enhance critical engagement.
Personal reflections on individual reverence are interesting but unsupported by evidence. The student should integrate examples or survey data to substantiate claims about personal belief practices.

The inclusion of a comparative image is promising, yet the student does not integrate it into the argument. They should explicitly discuss how this visual evidence challenges assumptions about certainty in classical sculpture.
The expert quote from Peckmeizian is used without contextualizing its authority or date. The student should situate the sourceâs credentials and explain its relevance to reinforce credibility.

The introduction of ballet positions presents tradition as unquestioned but lacks analysis of how these conventions were established. The student should examine the historical and cultural forces that confer reverence.
The assertion that these positions have ânever been questionedâ overlooks historical variation in dance pedagogy. Engaging with dissenting practices would provide a more balanced evaluation.
Reliance on a blog post for the ballet diagram raises concerns about source reliability. The student should incorporate primary historical texts or scholarly analyses to substantiate claims.
The student poses questions about reverence but stops short of exploring the aesthetic versus functional dimensions. They should contrast these elements to sharpen the evaluation.

The assertion that natural-science discoveries are ânot really ever doubtedâ is an overgeneralization. Incorporating examples of scientific paradigm shifts would nuance the claim and illustrate fragility within the sciences.

The claim that revered laws can be incompatible with reality is presented without engaging with the underlying mathematical or empirical grounds. The student should connect this to knowledge methods for depth.

Referring to Einsteinâs work as âtheory of relativismâ is inaccurate. The student should use correct terminology (âtheory of relativityâ) to maintain precision and demonstrate understanding.

The everyday example of the soccer ball clearly illustrates Newtonâs laws but stops short of evaluating their limitations. The student should discuss how relativistic or quantum contexts challenge these laws.

The opening of the conclusion restates the studentâs stance but does not synthesize insights from the analysis. The student should draw explicit connections between examples and overarching knowledge claims.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
